content_en:
The chapter on marriage in al-Umm is among al-Shafi'i's most thoroughly argued sections, addressing both the spiritual dimension of marriage as a divinely endorsed bond and the precise legal conditions that make a contract of marriage valid under Islamic law. Al-Shafi'i treats marriage (nikah) as a contract with specific pillars, and the absence of any one of them renders it void (batil) rather than merely irregular (fasid).

The pillars of a valid marriage in the Shafi'i school are five: the contracting guardian (wali), the prospective husband, the prospective wife, the two male witnesses, and the offer and acceptance (ijab wa qabul). Of these, the requirement of a guardian (wali) for the bride is one of the most defining features of the Shafi'i school. Al-Shafi'i holds that no woman, whether virgin or previously married, may contract her own marriage without the participation of her wali. This position is grounded in several hadiths, most prominently: 'There is no marriage except with a guardian,' which al-Shafi'i treats as an explicit condition rather than a recommendation.

The wali must be a sane, adult, free Muslim male from the bride's agnatic line — her father, then grandfather, then brothers, then uncles, in order of priority. A wali who is absent or refuses to give permission without valid reason may be bypassed by a judge, who acts as the guardian of last resort. Al-Shafi'i rejects the Hanafi position that an adult woman of sound mind may contract her own marriage independently, considering such a contract void regardless of how suitable the match may be.

Two male witnesses of sound character (adl) must be present at the time of the offer and acceptance for the marriage to be valid. A marriage contracted in secret or without witnesses is void in the Shafi'i school. This distinguishes Shafi'i law from the Maliki position, which requires public announcement (i'lan) but is more flexible about formal witnessing at the moment of contract.

Mahr, the obligatory bridal gift from the husband to the wife, is a right that belongs exclusively to the wife. Al-Shafi'i holds that mahr is not a pillar of the marriage contract — a marriage is valid even if no amount is specified, in which case a fair mahr (mahr al-mithl) based on the bride's social standing becomes obligatory. The wife has the right to withhold sexual access until the prompt portion of her mahr is paid.

The prohibited degrees of marriage are specified in the Quran and Sunnah and include blood relatives, foster relatives (milk kinship from being nursed by the same woman), and in-laws. Al-Shafi'i follows the hadith that 'suckling makes prohibited what birth makes prohibited,' applying milk kinship rules to any nursing that took place in infancy, regardless of the number of feedings. The Shafi'i school requires a minimum of five separate feedings to establish milk kinship, in line with a narration from Aisha that abrogated an earlier ruling.

Al-Shafi'i also addresses the marriage of a Muslim man to a free woman from the People of the Book — permissible with conditions — and the absolute prohibition on Muslim women marrying non-Muslim men. He discusses the rules governing polygyny up to four wives, including the obligation of equal treatment in material provision and overnight stays, though he acknowledges that equal treatment of the heart is beyond legal compulsion.
content_id:
Al-Umm membahas nikah (pernikahan) secara sangat komprehensif, mencerminkan keyakinan al-Syafi'i bahwa hukum keluarga Islam harus didasarkan sepenuhnya pada teks-teks Al-Quran dan hadis yang sahih, bukan semata pada tradisi atau adat.

Pembahasan nikah mencakup: anjuran menikah dalam Islam, syarat-syarat akad nikah yang sah (ijab-qabul, wali, saksi, mahar), wali nikah dan urutannya, mahar (mitsil dan musamma) dan ketentuannya, wanita-wanita yang haram dinikahi (mahram), nikah dengan non-Muslim (kafir dzimmi), pernikahan yang fasid dan konsekuensinya, hak-hak suami dan istri, nafkah (makanan, pakaian, tempat tinggal), nusyuz dan konsekuensinya, talak (raj'i dan ba'in), khul', ila', zhihar, li'an, iddah (wajib bagi perempuan dicerai atau ditinggal mati), dan hadanah (pengasuhan anak setelah perceraian).
content_ar:
باب الزواج في الأم من أكثر أبواب الشافعي احتجاجاً ومناقشةً، وهو يُعالج بُعدَي الزواج: البُعد الروحي باعتباره رابطة إلهية مباركة، والبُعد الفقهي المحدد بشروط دقيقة وأحكام مستفيضة.

ويُؤكد الشافعي اشتراط الوليّ في عقد الزواج: «لا نكاح إلا بولي»، محتجاً بالأحاديث الصريحة في ذلك. ومن اختصاصات موقفه: اشتراط الوليّ في زواج المرأة مطلقاً سواء أكانت بكراً أم ثيباً، وهو موقف يفترق به عن الحنفية الذين يُجيزون للبالغة الرشيدة مباشرة العقد.

والمهر يُعرض مع تمييز دقيق بين مهر المسمى ومهر المثل، وأحكام كل منهما في حالات الطلاق قبل الدخول وبعده. ومن الأحكام المميزة للمذهب: أن الطلاق قبل الدخول يُسقط نصف المهر المسمّى إذا كان قد سُمّي.
